title: Reciprocity

content.statement: Giving first creates a sense of obligation that can be returned.
content.question: What can I give first that would be genuinely valuable to my customers or partners?
content.equation: Reciprocal Pull = Gift Value × Authenticity × Relevance
content.mental_model: Door held open, many will hold the next one for you.
content.business_lens: Lead with valuable free content, audits, or samples; do not transactionalize the gift.
content.life_lens: Give without keeping score; relationships compound.
sop: Identify high-value low-cost gifts (content, audit, intro)
Track whether reciprocity emerges over time
Avoid manipulative reciprocity (cheap gift, big ask)
content.explainer: Cialdini documents reciprocity across cultures. The norm runs deep: people who receive small unexpected gifts return larger ones. Authentic giving works; manipulative reciprocity backfires once recognized.
questions: What can I give first that would be genuinely valuable to my customers or partners?
The last time you gave something away for free, was it something you'd actually want if you were the recipient, or just cheap for you to produce?
Which relationship in your business feels transactional right now because you asked before you gave?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Gift Value 7, Authenticity 9, Relevance 8. Score = (7 plus 9 plus 8) divided by 3 = 8.0. Higher means the gift is likely to be genuinely reciprocated; lower means it reads as a transaction and generates no real goodwill.
content.closing_statement: Give first without counting; the count happens anyway.

caption:
Principle Nº 299: Reciprocity

Giving first creates a sense of obligation that can be returned. Cialdini documents reciprocity across cultures.

Ask yourself: What can I give first that would be genuinely valuable to my customers or partners?
